guideline_references: BSR/BHPR: Guidelines for the management of polymyalgia rheumatica 2010
Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology — Polymyalgia rheumatica and giant cell arteritis (current)
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance test: the presentation is alarming (sudden severe bilateral girdle rigidity, bedbound, two-person assist, telephone-triage framing to 'rule out stroke / cord compression') BUT the source note's own acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (giant cell arteritis screened negative; distal neurological exam intact) and its disposition is routine outpatient (start oral prednisolone, arrange ESR/CRP, GP review in 3 days). Correct telehealth answer is to recognise the inflammatory proximal-girdle picture, tier T2 (prompt GP), and NOT over-triage to ED/000.

Important-not-to-miss mimic held on the sealed side: giant cell arteritis (vision-threatening) — the case tests whether the AI attaches PROMINENT mandatory GCA safety-netting (headache/scalp tenderness/jaw claudication/visual change -> 000/ED) rather than escalating the PMR itself.

Telehealth reprojection: source is a physically-present home visit (clinician took vitals, examined ROM, palpated temporal arteries, tested reflexes and administered/prescribed oral steroid). The AI cannot examine, take vitals without a device, run bloods, or prescribe — it recognises + arranges prompt in-person GP review to confirm and initiate treatment.
Anchoring/premature-closure trap: severe pain + immobilisation may pull toward an acute structural/neurological emergency or toward simple 'muscle strain / ageing' — both are wrong.
Age inferred as older adult (~72) and sex inferred female — both flagged. Source category: CFE (Complex Fatigue Entities), CFE-035.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
